---
title: ""
subtitle: ""
author: ""
date: ""
format:
  pdf:
    toc: false
    keep-tex: false
    fontsize: 10.5pt
---

# Overall Results

Table~\ref{tbl-overall-performance} compares the main finite-sample criteria with
estimators shown across columns.

```{=latex}
\input{../results/report_full_run/report_assets/table_overall_transposed.tex}
```

## Estimators

1. **Oracle IVQR** knows the true active support and isolates the cost of nuisance
   estimation and selection, but is infeasible outside simulation.
2. **Mean-Lasso Post-selection IVQR** selects controls from the data and then
   applies IVQR, providing a repository-specific feasible sparse comparison.
3. **DML-style IVQR** uses cross-fitted nuisance estimation and residualized scores to
   reduce first-order sensitivity to nuisance-learning error.

## Overall-results discussion

{{< include ../results/report_full_run/report_assets/overall_findings.md >}}

The ranking changes with the criterion. Oracle performs best for point accuracy;
the DML-style procedure performs closest to nominal average coverage in these
simulations; Mean-Lasso Post-selection IVQR is slightly more compact than Oracle
but inferentially less reliable.

# Monte Carlo Design

Table~\ref{tbl-design} records the validated design grid and the implementation
settings used by the completed full run.

```{=latex}
\input{../results/report_full_run/report_assets/table_design.tex}
```

{{< include ../results/report_full_run/report_assets/design_summary.md >}}

# Coverage Across Design Cells

Aggregate coverage can conceal heterogeneous cell-level performance.
Table~\ref{tbl-coverage-dispersion} therefore summarizes the distribution of the validated
coverage values across design cells before the subgroup comparisons.

```{=latex}
\input{../results/report_full_run/report_assets/table_coverage_dispersion_transposed.tex}
```

{{< include ../results/report_full_run/report_assets/coverage_dispersion_findings.md >}}

# Results by Quantile

@fig-coverage-quantile compares empirical coverage by structural quantile.
The vertical bars show 95% Monte Carlo uncertainty around each pooled coverage
estimate, while the dashed line marks the nominal 95% level.

![Empirical coverage by structural quantile. Error bars are normal 95% Monte Carlo intervals around pooled resolved-replication coverage; they do not establish uniform coverage across design cells.](../results/report_full_run/report_assets/figure_coverage_by_quantile.pdf){#fig-coverage-quantile width=94%}

@fig-bias-quantile and @fig-rmse-quantile show the corresponding point-
estimation behavior. Bias records direction, whereas RMSE combines dispersion
and bias and should not be interpreted as an inferential-validity measure.

![Bias by structural quantile. Values are computed from the validated quantile-level summary.](../results/report_full_run/report_assets/figure_bias_by_quantile.pdf){#fig-bias-quantile width=94%}

![RMSE by structural quantile. Lower RMSE indicates stronger point-estimation performance but does not guarantee calibrated confidence regions.](../results/report_full_run/report_assets/figure_rmse_by_quantile.pdf){#fig-rmse-quantile width=94%}

Table~\ref{tbl-quantile} reports the underlying quantile-specific metrics with
estimators arranged horizontally.

```{=latex}
\input{../results/report_full_run/report_assets/table_by_quantile_transposed.tex}
```

{{< include ../results/report_full_run/report_assets/quantile_findings.md >}}

# Results by Instrument Strength

@fig-coverage-strength shows coverage across the four instrument-strength
settings with Monte Carlo uncertainty and the nominal reference line.

![Empirical coverage by instrument-strength index. Error bars are normal 95% Monte Carlo intervals around pooled resolved-replication coverage; the coverage axis spans 0--100%.](../results/report_full_run/report_assets/figure_coverage_by_strength.pdf){#fig-coverage-strength width=94%}

@fig-length-strength and @fig-full-grid-strength separate localization
from calibration: shorter regions indicate more localization, while the full-grid
rate shows how often the reported region accepts the entire parameter grid.

![Mean confidence-region length by instrument-strength index. Region length is shown in parameter units, not percentages.](../results/report_full_run/report_assets/figure_cr_length_by_strength.pdf){#fig-length-strength width=94%}

![Percentage of replications with full-grid confidence regions by instrument-strength index.](../results/report_full_run/report_assets/figure_full_grid_by_strength.pdf){#fig-full-grid-strength width=94%}

Table~\ref{tbl-strength} reports the validated strength-specific metrics with
estimators as columns.

```{=latex}
\input{../results/report_full_run/report_assets/table_by_strength_transposed.tex}
```

{{< include ../results/report_full_run/report_assets/strength_findings.md >}}

# Results by Sample Size and Dimension

Table~\ref{tbl-n-p} compares the four sample-size and control-dimension combinations.

```{=latex}
\input{../results/report_full_run/report_assets/table_by_n_p_transposed.tex}
```

@fig-coverage-n-p adds Monte Carlo uncertainty to the same coverage
comparison without introducing another performance metric.

![Empirical coverage by sample size and control dimension. Error bars are normal 95% Monte Carlo intervals around pooled resolved-replication coverage; the coverage axis spans 0--100%.](../results/report_full_run/report_assets/figure_coverage_by_n_p.pdf){#fig-coverage-n-p width=94%}

# Coverage--Informativeness Trade-off

@fig-coverage-length compares coverage and mean confidence-region length at each
estimator--quantile aggregation. Within each estimator series, the connected
points follow the quantiles from 0.25 through 0.75; the dashed line marks nominal
coverage.

![Coverage versus mean confidence-region length by estimator and structural quantile. Vertical bars show normal 95% Monte Carlo intervals. Shorter regions are preferable only when calibration remains credible.](../results/report_full_run/report_assets/figure_coverage_length_tradeoff.pdf){#fig-coverage-length width=94%}

# Worst-Performing Design Cells

Tables~\ref{tbl-worst-cells-a} and~\ref{tbl-worst-cells-b} retain the ordinary row-oriented
layout because every row is a distinct design cell. The two panels use identical
row ordering to keep all requested fields readable.

```{=latex}
\input{../results/report_full_run/report_assets/table_worst_cells.tex}
```

{{< include ../results/report_full_run/report_assets/worst_findings.md >}}



@fig-boundary-estimates shows how often the initial-grid point estimate
falls on a boundary. It is a point-estimation diagnostic, not a coverage measure.

![Boundary-estimate rate by estimator. Rates are based on the validated overall summary and are shown on a 0--100% axis.](../results/report_full_run/report_assets/figure_boundary_estimates.pdf){#fig-boundary-estimates width=94%}

The selected-control variable is available for Mean-Lasso Post-selection IVQR.
@fig-selected-controls presents it only as a descriptive diagnostic;
variation in selection size is confounded with the underlying design dimensions.

![Mean-Lasso post-selection design-cell coverage versus the mean number of selected controls. Color denotes the structural quantile. The relationship is descriptive and does not identify a causal effect of selection size on coverage.](../results/report_full_run/report_assets/figure_post_selection_controls_vs_coverage.pdf){#fig-selected-controls width=94%}
